title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who took up a challenging new hobby. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Sam, Oil Painting
'I’d always been a technical person, so the fluidity of oil paint was terrifying,' Sam admits. [47]I spent the first month just staring at the blank canvas, paralyzed by the fear of making a permanent mistake.[/47] [53]I eventually realized that in painting, as in life, you can usually layer over your errors.[/53] [50]A turning point came when I stopped trying to replicate photographs and started trying to capture how the light actually felt.[/50] [55]It was a solitary pursuit that provided a much-needed escape from my hectic social life.[/55] Now, my studio is my sanctuary. The process of mixing colors is almost meditative for me, requiring a focus that shuts out all other daily worries and stresses.
texts.B:
### Naomi, Long-Distance Running
Naomi started running at fifty. 'I couldn't even reach the end of the street without gasping,' she recalls. [49]What kept me going was a local running club where the members were incredibly inclusive, regardless of pace.[/49] [52]I had underestimated the mental discipline required; it’s far more a battle of the mind than the legs.[/52] [48]I was pleasantly surprised when my chronic back pain began to diminish as my core strength improved.[/48] [56]I’d initially signed up just to lose weight, but the goal quickly shifted to simply seeing how far my body could go.[/56] Completing my first half-marathon was an emotional milestone I never thought possible. It has completely redefined my understanding of physical limits.
texts.C:
### Victor, Classical Guitar
Victor took up the guitar after retiring. 'My fingers felt like lead at first,' he says. [51]The most frustrating part was the discrepancy between what I could hear in my head and what my hands could produce.[/51] [54]I almost gave up when I realized that progress wasn't linear; I’d have weeks where I seemingly regressed.[/54] [50]However, the discipline of daily practice brought a sense of order to my retirement that I hadn't realized I was missing.[/50] [47]Initially, I was worried about what my neighbors would think of the constant scales, but they were actually very encouraging.[/47] It’s a lifelong journey, and I’ve learned to enjoy the slow, painstaking process of mastering a single complex piece of music.
texts.D:
### Lara, Pottery
Lara discovered pottery during a stressful period at work. 'There’s something very grounding about literal clay,' she observes. [49]I found that the online community of potters was a goldmine of technical advice and moral support.[/49] [52]I hadn't anticipated how much the hobby would drain my bank account, between kiln fees and specialized glazes.[/52] [56]I started the class to meet people, but I ended up falling in love with the solitude of the wheel.[/56] [53]Pottery taught me that perfection is overrated; often the most beautiful pieces are the ones with slight, 'human' imperfections.[/53] [48]I also noticed a significant improvement in my manual dexterity, which helped with my day job as a surgeon.[/48] It is a truly transformative experience.

questions.question: mentions a concern about how others might perceive their initial efforts?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) was worried about neighbors hearing his scales. Sam (A) also mentions fear of mistakes.

questions.question: mentions an unexpected physical benefit of their new hobby?
questions.explanation: Naomi (B) noticed her back pain disappeared, and Lara (D) noticed improved manual dexterity.

questions.question: highlights the importance of a supportive group of people?
questions.explanation: Naomi (B) mentions the inclusive running club and Lara (D) mentions the online community.

questions.question: notes how the hobby improved their overall sense of well-being or structure?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) says practice brought order to retirement. Sam (A) calls his studio a sanctuary.

questions.question: describes the frustration of a gap between mental ability and physical execution?
questions.explanation: Victor mentions the discrepancy between what he heard in his head and what his hands could do.

questions.question: admits to being caught off guard by a specific demand of the hobby?
questions.explanation: Naomi (B) underestimated the mental discipline, while Lara (D) hadn't anticipated the high costs.

questions.question: explains how the hobby changed their philosophical outlook on mistakes?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) realized he could layer over errors, and Lara (D) learned that perfection is overrated.

questions.question: mentions that their improvement was not always consistent?
questions.explanation: Victor says his progress wasn't linear and he had weeks where he 'seemingly regressed'.

questions.question: states that the hobby provided a contrast to their usual social environment?
questions.explanation: Sam says it was a solitary escape from a 'hectic social life'.

questions.question: acknowledges that their original motivation for starting changed over time?
questions.explanation: Naomi (B) started for weight loss but changed to testing limits. Lara (D) started to meet people but ended up liking the solitude.
